turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Miller, I think you may proceed whenever you are ready. please the Court: action that there are two Appellant groups, the first consisting of the labor organization, and the second consisting of state officials. We have agreed upon a single speaker to avoid fragmentation of our presentation. We do submit to the Court that the state officials and the labor organizations have both common this Court in reversing the lower court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which case are you from or in? MR. tlILLER: Your Honor, the clients that I represented in the action below were the defendant labor organizations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: The primary issue in this case law violates either the First or the Fourteenth Amendments where it requires a public employer to meet and confer only with an exclusive representative and that exclusive representative uses only its members as apart of the meet and confer meeting. In the alternative, should this Cou r t affirm is entitled to run and vote in elec t ions for membership on the meet and confer committees , do the First and Fourteenth Amendments require that a s ystem of cumulative voting be utilized in those elections. The fundamental facts in the case are neither extensive nor in dispute. The Plaintiffs are individual faculty members employed on several of the campuses of the 18-campus system in the Minne s ota Community These individual faculty member Plaintiffs are opposed to having the defendant labor organziations represent them concerning their employment interests. The defendant labor organizations consist of the Minnesota Community College Faculty Association, the MCCFA, which is a voluntary association of faculty members within the Community College system. It is the legally certified exclusive representative for all of the faculty members within the Community College system. Its primary purpos e is to represent the employ- ment interest of all of the faculty members . The Minnesota Education Association is a is the state affiliate for the MCCFA . The National Education Association is a voluntary association of educators which is the national affiliate of the· MCCFA and the MEA. The Community College Board is a state agency of Minnesota charged with the management of the Community College system. It is the employer of the faculty members involved in this action. Our Public Employment Labor Relations Act system for labor relations for public employers and public employees. Its stated policies of PELRA are to promote order ly and constructive relationships between public employers and public employees; that unresolved disputes between employers and employees are injurious to both the parties as well as the public. The policy section further specifically indicates that one of the policies is to grant the employees the PELRA specifically provides for a democratic selection of those representatives in the form t hat an election may take place every two years to determine who the exclusive representative for the faculty members in this particular bargaining unit shall be . The fundamental framework of PELRA to achieve these policies is the use of the concept of exclusive representation of employees . All exclusive representa- tives under PELRA are obligated to meet and negotiate, that is collectively bargain the terms and conditions of employment. Those terms and conditions of employment are defined expressly in PELRA as being the hours of employment, compensation, and the personnel policies affecting the working conditions of the employees. PELRA specifically indicates that there are subjec t s which are not to be subjecte? to the collective bargaining process. That, in our state statute, is referred to as inherent managerial policy . Those are areas of discretion in policy such as the overall budget, the organizational structure of the employer, and the selection and direction of personnel . Thus, there are two major arenas of activity for an exclusive representative; that is negotiable areas and non-negotiable areas, and specifically as to professional employees in Minnesota the exclusive representative is charged to meet and confer with the public employer concerning areas within the inherent management discretion a rea. This is a right that is not extended to other public employees other than professional public employees in Minnesota. with them? MR . MILLER: The statute requires that it meet at a minimum every four months with the repre- sentative of the professional employees for meet and confer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could the employer meet with somebody else too?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: The employer could meet --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Separately? case, the employer has met regularly with both the individual Plaintiffs as well as other faculty members .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could he have them in the same meeting with -- Cou ld he have them at the same meeting when he meets with the exclusive representative?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: I think they could and that has taken place. The record will indicate that in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This statute is really about the legislature of Minnesota directing its agents whom to meet with, isn't it?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: That is correct. This is a statutorily created righc. That is the right of meet and confer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It requires them to meet with one group and its option with another, I suppose.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: It is optional with another group. There is, very frankly, Justice White, the question of whether or not the employer possibly com- mitting an unfair labor practice in doing that. We do not believe though that that raises a constitutional questions as I will explain in a moment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean it is -- I thought you said under the state law, if the employer chose to meet with other groups, he could without violating any state law .

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: The purpose of meet and confer, we believe, is private consultation not only to permit employees to freely express their views, which, of course, is their First Amendment right, and has been confirmed in this record as well as under PELRA. Meet and confer obligates the employer to listen and respond

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To the exclusive representative under --

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, may he meet and listen to others besides the exclusive representative?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he wouldn't be committing an unfair labor practice.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Your Honor, I think there could be a question

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under state law.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Under state law as to at what point an employer meeting and conferring with someone else other than the exclusive representative may be violating

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is what I was asking you awhile ago. Now, what is your answer to the question. Is he in violation of the state law if he meets with other people or not?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: I believe the answer is going to be dependent on whether or not that employer, in fact, is exchanging views and holding private consulta- tion as opposed to simply receiving the views of individual employees.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, is there a difference in subject matter between bargaining issues and meet and confer issues?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Most definitely. What is the definition? the private sector, it is a gray and moving line . it may be a matter of inherent management right and And, we have cited several decisions in our brief that indicate how that definition of a meet and negotiate subject is evolving in our state. There is no bright, clear line. The point that I want to make in response to your inquiry, Justice White, is that that line does not connotate, as the lower court did , some type of constitutional right, because it is a line that is drawn -- way I look at the case as to whether or not, under state law, anybody but the -- that the employer would violate state law if he meets on meet and confer issues with anybody but the exclusive representative.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: I believe the employer can, either at a public meeting or as indicated in this record in sessions held on campus --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Say he does -- Say he meets with he goes through exactly the same routine as he does with the exclusive representative, and if you say he must just listen, then he just listens. Now, if that is all he does, he is all right under state law?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: I believe so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, I understood from what you said earlier that the Board could make no response on any proposals that were suggested by the non-union meet and confer group . make a response, but the question, I believe, Your Honor, is not whether or not that causes some con- stitutional infringeme nt , but whether or not it is an unf a ir l a bor practice .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Apart from the cons titutional issue, I think the factual question that I understood Justice White was mak ing inquiry about , is what actually happens? What sort of utility would be What purpose would be served by such a meeting if all t h e Board sat there mum while people presented ide a s a nd there was no discussion, no suggestions, no advice? Would that ser ve any purpose?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: The purpose of the s t atute is to obtain the majori ty view of the facu l t y in an orde rly and constructive fashion .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, that would come form the exclusive representative. MR . MILLER: Yes, indeed, it would.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well , let's put it this way. Suppose the employer meets with a group other than the exclusive representative. Can't he do with that group on meet and confer issues precisely what he could do wi th the exclusive r epresentative?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Yes. It may freely participate in dialogue --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Whatever kind of a dialogue the statute contemplates on meet and confer issues between the exclusive representative and the employer, the employer can engage in with others. It is exactly the same procedure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is not what I understood you to say earlier.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You changed your --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I agree.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Your Honor

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I think before it would be an unfair labor practice or arguably it would be an unfair labor practice.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: The question is hinged on the fact that the exclusive representative has by statute, being the majority representative, the opportunity to present the majority view. In our view, there is nothing to restrict the employer from receiving the views of other employees as well.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even on matters in the contract.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, no.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: I am sorry?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean on matters that are not covered by the contract, don't you? individual employees have the right to express their views so long as it does not circumvent the rights of t he exclusive representative . That right is the opportunity to meet and confer, private consultation, in regard to inherent management rights .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why would it interfere with your rights to present your views on management issues?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: It does not , Your Honor. The prese ntation --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For the employer to have a meeting and listen to somebody else.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: The presentation of those views consti t utes no problem. I am sorry for any confusion on that issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You still .don ' t tell us very much about responding to the presentation. That presents the problem. QUESTION : Can they negotiate? MR . MILLER: The answer is no. There may not be any negotiation, just as there cannot be any negotiation by a non-certified representative in collective bargaining, meet and negotiate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Arguably there may be no response. MR . MILLER: Under meet and negotiate

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, under meet and confer.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: -- the employer cannot --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under meet and confer your view is t hat arguably there can be no respon s e to a non-union presentation of views . MR . MILLER: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, isn't that also true of a meet and confer presentation by the certified repre- sen t ative? That isn' t a negotiating s e s sion. MR . MILLER : It is not .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, is there much d i fference in the kind of response that an employer would give to two different meet and confer sessions , one with minority or representatives of the other wi t h the certified representative? MR. MILLER : No. In fact, Jus tice Rehnquist, the employer may decide to simply receive the views of the exclusive representative without discussing them with the exclusive representative, because, again , the employer has the final decision-making power concerning those non-negotiable matters. Those are matters of policy which they are not required or compelled to come to any agreement upon.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What you are saying -- Let's see if I can get it clear for myself. The empl oyer can listen to anyone. coherent fashion for the employer to receive the views dissident members at a formal meet and confer session with the views of the majority would very well disrupt that orderly process just as it would if the dissident minority members were permitted to be present at a private bargaining session and indicate disagreement with the exclusive representative concerning its position on what the terms and conditions ought to be. of the once every-four- month meetings, the answer is, no , they do not have the same status . The exclusive representat ive in this case, the MCCFA, h a s been demo- For purposes of that meet and confer meeting, the employe r has es t ablished that it wishes to hear the views only in that particular mode of the exclusive representative . It does not restrict that employer, in a s ubsequent meeting immediately af t er a meet and confe r meeting , then in receiving the views of all other employees concer ning any matter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Has there ever been such a meeting, a subsequent meeting, some ad hoc group? MR. MILLER : Yes. The record is replete that the Community College Board , the Chancellor , who is the Chief Executive Officer, the local presidents and other administrators have regularly held town forums for the purpose of obtaining the views of all other emp l oyees on all matters that are incl uded under meet and confer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, suppose half a dozen faculty members were designated as a committee representing the 18 non-union faculty members who are involved in this case as I understand it and requested the opportunity on specified dates regularly to present their views would the consequence of that request?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: None whatsoever. They are certainly entitled to do that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any authorities to support

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: In terms of their ability to

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: I would believe that the decision of this Court in City of Madison would directly support that, a meeting called for the purpose of obtaining the input of either the faculty or the public at large, that they would be guaranteed their First Amendment freedom of expressing their views concerning all subjects under meet and confer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The employer wouldn't have to meet with them?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: If it calls a public meeting, Your Honor, I think it certainly would.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, if it called a public meeting, but the difference here is that the law requires the employer to meet with the exclusive representative

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: That is correct .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- and doesn't require them to meet at all with anybody else.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: It is exactly right. The First Amendment does not require, and this Court has so held, a public employer to meet and negotiate, meet and respond with any public employee. The converse of that is that a public employer, therefore, is free to privately consult with whomever they choose. In this case, the legislature in Minnesota has decided that the employer shall meet and confer in private consultation with the designated exclusive representatives of the professional employees.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is even that quite right? Does the statutory provision dealing with meet and confer expressly refer to the exclusive bargaining agent? Could it not be consistent with the statute for the majority for meet and confer purposes to elect someone else?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: No, I don't believe so, Your Honor. The statute provides that the professional employees shall select a representative.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. But, does it have to be the same representative that represents them for the other purpose?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Yes. Another portion of the statute specifically provides that the employer shall not meet and negotiate or meet and confer with any employee or group of employees other than the exclusive representative if one has been certified. That is 179.66, Subdivision 7 . representative. to meet and confer which means private consultation. It can be an unfair labor practice. That is the distinction we have been trying to draw. No First The difference does not go off on any First Amendment right, because no individual employee or group of employees has the right to compel government to meet and discuss and respond to any subject. And, the state has created that right for exclusive representatives just as it has for exclusive representatives in meet and negotiate. The statutory plan very simply is that all professional employees may elect in a democratic procedure any exclusive representative. The statute specifically provides that the employer shall not meet and negotiate or meet and confer with anyone, any employee or group of employees other than the designated exclusive representati\ if there be one. If there not be one, the answer to Justice Stevens' question is the professional employees would be free to select a representative, but if there is an exclusive representative, the employer must meet and confer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, as I understand it, the minority or non-union people can only communicate when such a communication is a part of the employee's work assign- ment.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: No, that I don't believe --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What does that mean that is in Subdivision 7?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: That is an amendment to the statute which I believe is designed to protect the employer that it will not be committing an unfair labor practice to receive the views of an individual employee concerning matters that are part of their work assignment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That employee's work assignment?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, if he just didn't think the tuition was right in the school, he couldn't talk about that.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: I think he certainly can.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: As a matter of First Amendment freedom. exclusive bargaining agent lead you to qualify any of your earlier answers about what the employer could do with respect to meetings with people other than the certified bargaining representative?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: No, I don't believe so, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I just don't understand

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I can't figure it out.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I can't either.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It seems to me you have given answers at 360 degrees.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Well, I am certainly not trying to, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know you are not .

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Let me try my hand one more time. The principle of exclusive representation has been decreed in Minnesota to permit professional employees to be able to not only meet and negotiate but also to meet and confer. That meet and confer means the exchange of views and concerns. The purpose is to obtain the majority view of the majority repre- sen ta ti ves.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Inherent management policy, matters that are not in collective bargaining. However, that line, as I tried to indicate before, is a gray and uncertain one. An example: In the collective bargaining contract, class size, which has been negotiated, is said that it must be reasonable. However, the actual class sizes are determined through the meet and confer process on the individual campuses as to how large the classes shall be. If an administration wants to change the class size, it must do it through advance notice and meet and confer. If there is a violation of the class size that has been determined through meet and confer, it is grievable under the contract. There is a very close interrelationship between -- for professional employees between those sub j ects that are in meet and confer and those subjects that are meet and negotiate which is to say that those subjects may cross the line, depending on the wishes of the party or the will of the legislature, because the legislature could decree that there are no inherent management subjects and everything would come in under collective bargaining. There would be no private right of meet and confer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The administrator would have a terrible job trying to know which hat he has on, wouldn't he?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: It might very well.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Practically daily, wouldn't it?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Well, only with the exclusive to meet and negotiate concerning a certain subject or whether it is an inherent management policy right that i s not subject to the collective bargaining obligations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, could you tell me the difference between meet and confer and negotiate?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Meet and negotiate are those subjects which relate to the terms and conditions of employment. what goes on.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Meet and negotiate involves negotiating a salary schedule, negotiating a vacation .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When you meet and confer you are not negotiating?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, because

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are not?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: No, you are not. The subjects of the public employer. After having met and conferred over those subjects which are properly in meet and

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Once you decide that the subject is meet and confer or this is the subject of negotiating, you do the same thing?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, meeting with the exclusive representative.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now I am lost.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Maybe we can try one more approach.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Good.

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: And that is the question of budget. The statute specifically says that a public employer's budget is not subject to meet and negotiate; that is no employee organization may attempt to negotiate over what the size of the budget shall be or how the budget shall be broken down. However, quite obviously, when it comes to negotiating a salary schedule, how much of the budget that will go toward the salary schedule is clearly a subject of meet and negotiate. In Minnesota, the subject of budget has regularly been involved in meet and confer with the exclusive representative to permit the majority representative to indicate to the employer its views concerning the size of th( budget and the utilization of the budget, but that process is not binding on the employer once the employer has met and conferred with the exclusive representative. shall not meet and confer with anyone e xcept the union?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: That is true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So they can't talk to a dissident group about the budget?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: They cannot --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The cannot have a meet and confer session with the dissident group about the budget?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: Exactly, Your Honor . They certainly may --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is the state interest that is served by that prohibition?

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: The state interest is the same as it is for exclusive representation and meet and negotiation. That is to permit the employer to have the majority view, to eliminate conflicting demands by various groups of employees upon the employer, to eliminate the tension between employees and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: None of which are bargainable subjects?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Surely it does .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: St atutory prohibi tion against expr ession of divergen t views in comparable meetings . MR. MILLER : In a comparable meeting.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, the state policy is we want to hear one voice on the budget, not two voices. MR . MILLER : They want to hear the majority view on the state budget in a meet a nd confer session , that i s true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And nobody else .

turns.name: MILLER
turns.text: In that session that is right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or in a compar able session either, in t hat session or any o ne patterned afte r i t . MR . MILLER : I want to make myself hopefully , finally, and forever very clear. An individual faculty member has the First Amendment right if the individual admin istrator i s willing -- has the time , not because of PELRA -- to hear o u t that individual faculty member conce r ning his views on the budget. QUESTION : You are j ust saying that the state statute then i s unconstitutional.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because it prevents precisely what you just said the First Amendment guarantees. and confer in Section 179 . 63, Subdivision 15 , is, and employers and their respective employees," rather a The obl i gation of public employers in Section 179 . 66 , Subdivision 3 , is the obligation to meet and confer with professional employees to discuss policies and those matters relating to employment not included under Subsection 18 of 179.63. That is the matters of inherent managerial policy. Section 179.66, Subdivision 7, then tells the public employer that if an exclusive representative has been selected, the public employer shall not meet with any person or representative other than the exclus i ve representative; that is the statute creates an expansive definition of meet and confer as the exchange of views, obligates the public employer to meet with public employees to discuss this broad subject matter .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Vieira, you speak as though the public employer and the Minnesota legislature were two different entities. I don't see this case as any different than if each of these public employers, each of these presidents of Bemidji Junior College or whatever it might be, decided on their own to do exactly what the statute said. They are all creatures of the state and when the state tells them to do something, they do it.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: That is right, sir. I believe if they decided on their own to set up a policy in terms of regulation or a practice in these particular community colleges, we would have the same First Amendment and Fourteenth Amendment problems.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It doesn't remove the First Amendment analysis at all, but I just question your -- Perhaps I drew the wrong implication from what you are saying as if these administrators would somehow complain about, the administrators rather than the faculty members could complain about being imposed upon by the state. MR. VIEIRA : No, sir. I think it is clear t hat the legislature is creating a system involving the administrators which is intended , or at least has been applied -- let me not say intended, because I believe there is a way this statute could have been applied constitutionally if the Board and the MCCFA had chosen to do so. But, this statute has enabled them to apply the meet and confer process in a systemat ically dis- criminatory fashion against all non-members of the MCCPA for

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Vieira, if there had been no statute at all and you had a school board adminis- trator who simply decided that he was willing to confer with a single faculty member whom he liked and admired and he wasn't going to talk to anybody else on matters relating to the subject of the meet and confer law and just chose to talk to that individual . Is that a First Amendment violation of the rights of any other faculty member with whom the administ rator does not choose to confer? MR. VIEIRA : As a matter purely of his personal interest in the views of this individual or seeking the views of this individual for the purpose of somehow effecting or influencing the exercise of his official powers? MR. VIEIRA : Oh, yes, absolutely. Absolutely . I think once he opens up the process of conferring to any one of those faculty members, he has an obligation, unless he can come up with at least a rationale distinction among them for opening it up to all of them.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What case supports that argument?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, I am talking about the equal protection clause . That is at a minimum we have has suggested. Some faculty members are not allowed to discuss in an attempt to influence the public employer's exercise of his duties and this one particular --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They can attempt but he won't listen.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is no case -- facto condition to the grant of this right --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What are you talking about, an ex pos t facto condition in a civil case? For the The e x post facto clause applies only to c r iminal cases .

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: I wasn't using i t in that sense. I was using it in after the fact, in sense of the logical construction of this statute was first to set up the concept of meet and confer as the deside ratrum of the legislature and then to come on afterwa r ds and say if an exclusive representative has been selected, that representative will be the meet and confer representative, because you will notice the statute says if an exclusive representative has not been selected, never t heless, meet and confer wi ll go forward through s ome representa - tiona l process not involving

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is the discrimination you are talking about? MR. VIEIRA : Well, the discrimination in t his case, as the statute has been appl ied , is that t he only individuals among the faculty members of the community colleges who have been permitted to participat e in any manner whatsoever in the selection of meet and confer committees or serving on the meet and confer committees have been members of the MCCFA .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know, but that -- I wouldsuppose you would still be attacking the statute on the same grounds if the meet and confer committees had included non-union members as well . If that is the discrimina tion you are talking about, that is certainly different may talk to the employer. After all, the people the statute says the employer must meet with are the elected representatives, are the exclusive repre s e n tat i ves . They are then not just faculty members , they have been elected as exclusive representatives and they represent everybody in the bargaining unit, don't they?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, how is it discriminatory at all?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, the question here is whether or not we have a policy justification for limiting the individuals who can actually participate in the meet and confer session.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under the statute , how do you -- Who goes to the meet and confer meetings now the way they are run?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: The way they were run.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The way -- Who goes to the Yes, the way they were run, the way that you objected to. MR. VIEIRA : Well, there is certainly a possibility whereby this statute could be applied through the language of the statute. Public employers shall not meet and confer with public employees except through the exclusive representative. It doesn't say only with the exclusive representative. They could have of the faculty members to participate in t he selection of the members of the mee t and confer c o mmittees. In f ac t , prior

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well , they now do, i s that r ight? MR . VIEIRA : Excuse me, ma ' am .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now non-members can vote for the repr e s entative. MR. VIEIRA : Under t he Distri ct Court ' s o r de rs non - me mbers are allowed t o par t ici pa t e i n the voting process and they are allowed to stand for election .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Bu t your adversarie s challenge that on appeal, don't they? MR. VIEIRA : Oh , yes, sir , they d e finitely challenge that remedy . There is no quest i on about it . Historically, if one goes back, as the District Court recites in its findings and i t s opinion, you had a faculty qovernmen t system in t h es e Minnesota community colleges prior to the applica t ion of PELRA ' s meet and confer structure and that faculty government system involved all of the members of the faculty equally participating in voting, all having an equal opportunity at least to set themselves forward as candidates . This statute , if we can look at it in a pract ical sense, really in the aca demic context codified t hat s ystem . These professional employees are now goin g to have a statutory mechanism for meeting and conferring with their emp l oye r s . We have the same thing they did prior to the Public Employment Relation s Act, discussing academic standards, academic policies , the question of inherent managerial duti es. But, the statute added a new wrinkle and the new wrink le was tha t t h is advantageous condition , the elec t ion of an exclus ive representative for the purpose of meet and negot iate was now going to, as it has been applied at least, block off from participating in faculty govern- ments one distinct class of faculty members .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Vieira, I wond er if the state wasn't operating on a premise that dese r ves a little more recognition than I think you give i t . It is one thing to have a faculty senate get together and pass a bunch of resolutions, but I remember the remark of a colleague of mine in a former employment who had been a member of a law faculty. He said that the only thing worse than the meetings of the law faculty were the meetings of a full faculty, that the meetings of a full faculty simply never ended. If you are talking about a meeting of people to get together and pass a resolution when everyone there is voluntarily present it is one thing, but to require the administrators to sit and attend something, I think the state has a right to say you channel it, you don't have to listen to every single individual. Now, that is not interfering with the right of petition, on the scene and kind of reacting. He can't be there for five or six days. MR. VIEIRA : Well, we are making no argument that is inconsistent with that view, Justice Rehnquist. We are not attacking the structure of meet and confer in the sense that it sets up a regularized procedure whereby individuals are chosen to serve on these committees. We are saying that is fine. If the state wants to have such a regularized procedure, that is excellent, but at least have all of the professional employees in the relevant unit allowed to participate in the selection of those representatives and allowed to stand for election. The Board in this case will meet with the same number of meet and confer committees as were extant prior to the District Court's order. on the particular individual community college campuses, the same meet and confer committees will function. The only difference will be the composition within those committees. From the Board's point of view, it makes absolutely no difference. They are running the system precisely the way it has always been run . They are following the same orderly regularized procedure . From MCCFA's position, it makes a great deal of difference because now people will have a different perspective on academic questions, now people whom the Chief Justice perhaps correctly called dissidents in the sense that they dissent from MCCFA's policies, now those individuals will have some small voice in the official mechanism of communication to their employers. And that is what is at the heart of this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, you are not saying then that every faculty member has a right to be present and speak at this meet and confer session? You are only saying that they should have a right to cast a vote for the election of the representative who is there and

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Absolutely and to stand personally for election if they can receive sufficient votes. That is what we asked the District Court to declare and that is what the District Court -- employer said, well, you people who are disaffected representatives too separately. You go ahead and get together and elect me a half a dozen people and the next day after I meet with the exclusive representative I will meet with your representatives. practice. I think the statute precludes that. I think the employer is under a duty not to do that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know, but suppose the statute were declared unconstitutional in its present form and instead of having an election like the District Court, it said the employer should meet with the repre- sentatives of the minority. Would that satisfy you?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, it might very well satisfy me, but I don't know how this Court or

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I am only asking you. The District Court rewrote it at your behest considerably I must say .

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: I think it simply struck the exclusive representative's prerogative --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean that whole voting system is just simply striking something? MR . VIEIRA: Well, initially -- Recall initially all the District Court said was that the MCCFA had to hold a selection process whereby all the faculty members could participate equally. It was only when the MCCPA and the Board developed their so-called vote-for- six rule which required an individual, if he was voting for one of the dissidents, also to vote for some of the union members . The Court took a second look at this and said, well

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And rewrote the statute a little

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Excuse me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And rewrote the statute a little more.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, I think they created a remedy that maybe somewhat extraordinary in the sense that we can't find a direct. precedent for it , but it derives from the conduct that had to be dealt with.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if it derived a remedy, not in that form but in the form of saying that the employer shall meet with the committee representing the minority?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, we are perfectly willing to accept the proposition that the employer would be burdened to the extent that he has to meet or it has to meet with more than one committee.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, would you be litigating then if he agreed to do that ? sense?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, no, no, separately.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: What I mean is meet and confer is this process whereby the employer treats this input as something more than simply advantageous statements from a friendly faculty member, but as a view of a

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: And, if he meet with these people --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And on matters that are that are not wages, hours, and working conditions, the usual collective

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Yes, s i r, on inherent managerial

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Vieira, are you basically satisfied with the order of the District Court?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: I think the order of the District Court remedies the meet and confer problem that we had which was the inability of the non-union members to participate in any meaningful -- in any way whatsoever in the process. I can live with it. I can accept. one go beyond that . I would just point out one thing with respect to Mr. Miller's emphas is on the fundamenta li ty of this exclusivity principle here . The purpose of this statute, as stated in 179.73, is not to obtain simply a majority view. It is to obtain the view of all of the professional employees. I think this is particularly critical in an academic setting where it is quite clear that most faculty members are chosen for their complementary to the whole, each one fulfilling hopefully an unique nitch in the system. It really is not terribly rational to super- impose on that type of a system some majoritarian theory And, that is why the District Court, and nature of the communications and issues that are involved in this case. we are not, as the Chief Justice, I believe, correctly emphasized, dealing with wages, hours, and those other terms and conditions of employment that fall within the meet and negotiate category, those objective questions. The District Court pointed out, again I believe correctly, that in the area of meet and confer you are dealing with intangible questions inherently and when you are dealing in an academic setting you are dealing with questions that involve rather personal interactions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's just assume that the meet and negotiate provisions are valid and that the employer may be required to negotiate only with the exclusive representative and that he is forbidden to meet with anybody else and negotiate on those things. Now, if that is a valid situation scheme, why isn't this one?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, I think there are a number of reasons. The first one is the subject matter of these two areas is completely different.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I would think you would think the meet and negotiate subject matter would be far more important to you than the other.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, we do. We appealed that question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you lost it.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, it wasn't argued. We didn't brief it. But, the subject matter is -- taken that case and affirmed it. Say we just affirmed, that that was a completely constitutional scheme on meet and negotiate items to exclude anybody but the exclusive representative.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, of course

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why wouldn't that govern this case?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Because for two reasons. First, the subject matter is different. This Court is the source of the distinction in First Amendment jurisprudence between wages, hours, and working conditions, the collective bargaining trilogy, and other matters. If we go back to the Abood case, we discover that for some reasons collective bargaining politics is different from other forms of politics. All right. For purposes of analysis I accept that kind of distinction. Well, let's use it here. It seems to me there is greater distinction between wages, hours, and working conditions subject to exclusivity and discussion with a public employers, an arm of govern- ment, over inherent managerial policy questions. I think if you can say somehow wages, hours, and working terms of talking about their political ramifications and the rights of individuals to be coerced into supporting that kind of activity, then on the other side you would have to say the inherent managerial questions have to be looked at somewhat more seriously. have much more freedom to choose whom he wanted to talk with with respect to inherent managerial things than about wages, hours, and working conditions.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: But, they have chosen.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: The statute says --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you think it is unconstitutional.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, if you are getting to the question of whether the exclusivity principle can be carried over, the first problem, from my perspective in looking at it, would be that there is no procedural similarity in the two situations. That is if you accept the validity of the concept of collective bargaining through exclusivity, it does hinge ultimately, it seems to me, on the practical necessity of having one collective bargaining agreement.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If this statute was completely silent on the meet and confer aspect, had just the traditional labor relation negotiating provisions, is there anything that you know of in federal or state law on a committee or any group of employees they wa nted to discuss the subjects of management that you are talking about and policies if they wanted to do it?

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, I think if you carry that princi ple to its logic of conclusion , you run into a whole host of what I would call one-man, one-vote or political equality cases and a number of First Amend- ment cases that essentially teach

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think the First Amendment allowed the Minnesota administrators to talk with anyone they want about problems of management including their own employees, not only statute .

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, if they had a rational work-related reason for consulting with particular employees about particular management problems, that is one thing. We are not attacking the concept that employers should be allowed to talk with employees.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, no employer is here attacking the Minnesota statute on that basis either. This is an attack by people who want to talk to the employer, not an attack by the employer who claims his First Amendment rights are being denied.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: That is correct. I agree with you one hundred percent. The employer is on the other side. But, all the people here, all of the employees on both sides are in the same position with respect to this rational basis analysis. All of these employees are similarly situated with respect to the employer about their work assignments or the managerial questions go on in meet and confer. The distinction that is being made in this statute is here we have a private organizations, MCCFA, that has been selected for this purpose, negotiation, which concededly requires one separate negotiating entity. And, carried over from this we discover the application of this statute to preclude all the non-members which they all could participate equally and did prior to PELRA participate equal l y without any necessity for having a single private organization determine the content of those committees. That is why I can't understand their complaint that somehow the result that the District Court came up with is an interference, number one, with the meet and confer system, because the meet and confer system operates in essence the same way it always did with respect to the employer. Or, number two, that somehow it delegates from the position of this union as the exclusive represen tat ive with respect to negotiations. They are the representative with negotiations and the meet and confe r committees form the same structure, at least with respect to the Board, as they did prior to the District Court ' s order. What is the difference? The difference is the dissident s have come out to say something to their own employer and to t heir own government. That is the difference. They have been allowed to do the thing that before PELRA came into existence was taken for granted as part of the right of an academic. you why you needed any statute at all to have meet and confer sessions under the Minnesota statute.

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, excuse me, Your Honor . That is right. I think as a practical matter you wouldn't. I think any enlightened administrator running a community college or other system of higher education would have these interactions with the faculty.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, the meet and confer statute limits the authority of the administrators to discuss. I mean , that is what this case is all about really, isn't it? union. How are you going to blame the s t ate for that? organization the authority to do that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The decision to exclude your client,

turns.name: VIEIRA
turns.text: Well, it was a private decision acquiesced by this Board. It was a private decision

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, gentlemen, the case is submitted.
